questions.question: 1. What is the primary goal of the K-Means clustering algorithm?
options: A) To predict a continuous target number based on historical training data.
B) To group unlabeled data points into k distinct, non-overlapping clusters based on their similarities.
C) To classify data into pre-defined categories using labeled examples.
D) To find the actual data point that is most central in a dataset.

questions.question: 2. During the "Update" step of the standard K-Means algorithm, how is the new center (centroid) of a cluster calculated?
options: A) By finding the median value of the data points.
B) By selecting a random data point from the cluster.
C) By calculating the mathematical mean (average) of all data points currently assigned to that cluster.
D) By counting the number of categorical mismatches.

questions.question: 3. Why is standard K-Means highly sensitive to outliers?
options: A) Because outliers cannot be assigned to any cluster and break the algorithm.
B) Because calculating a mathematical average pulls the cluster center heavily toward extreme values.
C) Because the algorithm automatically deletes outliers, resulting in lost data.
D) Because it uses Manhattan distance instead of Euclidean distance.

questions.question: 4. What specific mathematical value is the K-Means algorithm trying to minimize?
options: A) The total number of clusters (k).
B) The Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS), also known as Inertia.
C) The distance between different cluster centroids.
D) The Silhouette Score.

questions.question: 5. What is a major risk of initializing K-Means with completely random starting centroids?
options: A) The algorithm will crash and fail to run.
B) You may get completely different final clusters every time you run the algorithm, sometimes resulting in poor groupings.
C) The algorithm will automatically switch to K-Medoids.
D) It guarantees finding the absolute best global clustering solution.

questions.question: 6. Which technique is commonly used to solve the "bad random initialization" problem in K-Means by smartly spreading out the initial centers?
options: A) K-Modes
B) The Elbow Method
C) K-Means++
D) CLARA

questions.question: 7. When using the "Elbow Method" to find the optimal number of clusters (k), what are you looking for on the graph?
options: A) The highest possible peak on the chart.
B) The exact point where the WCSS (Inertia) drops to zero.
C) The point where the WCSS curve sharply bends and the rate of decrease slows down significantly.
D) The point where the line perfectly intersects the X-axis.

questions.question: 8. Standard K-Means assumes that the natural groupings in your data are shaped like what?
options: A) Long, intertwining lines.
B) Spheres or circles (convex shapes).
C) Concentric rings.
D) U-shapes.

questions.question: 9. Which distance metric is the standard choice used by K-Means to calculate the distance between a data point and a centroid?
options: A) Manhattan Distance
B) Cosine Similarity
C) Mismatch Score
D) Euclidean Distance

questions.question: 10. What type of data does standard K-Means fail to process correctly?
options: A) Continuous numerical data (like Height or Salary).
B) Pure categorical data (like "Red", "Blue", or "Square").
C) Large datasets with thousands of rows.
D) Data containing negative numbers.
